content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 2, 2018

Cathy Capriola
City Manager
City of Sonoma
No. 1 The Plaza
Sonoma, CA 95476-6618

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-029

Dear Ms. Capriola:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Vice Mayor Amy Harrington
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note
that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under
other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section
1090. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

May Vice Mayor Harrington participate in governmental decisions regarding granting funds
to the Sonoma Community Center (the "SCC") and an update of the City's community fund policy
to move from annual grants to multiyear contracts, despite having a real property interest located
within 500 feet of the property owned by the SCC?

CONCLUSION

Yes. Vice Mayor Harrington may participate in governmental decisions regarding the
granting of funds to the SCC and an update to the City's community fund policy.

FACTS

The SCC is an Internal Revenue Code 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization founded in 1952.
Since its formation, the SCC has owned and maintained a historic former grammar school building
(the "Center"), a 32,000 square-foot structure that includes large conference rooms, smaller rooms,

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-029
Page No. 2

a commercial kitchen, office spaces, art fabricating facilities, parking, and a 99-seat theater for the
performing arts. The SCC rents some of its rooms to businesses, artists, musicians and others for
various purposes. The SCC's administrative offices are located in the Center. Theatrical and
musical performances have been conducted at the Center for many decades. Other events have been
held at the Center, including art exhibits and fund-raising events. The SCC also offers classes and
workshops in fine arts, ceramics, music, theater, and culinary arts. You note that Vice Mayor
Harrington lives across the street and within 62 feet of the property owned by the SCC, and that her
interest in her residence exceeds $2,000 in value.

The SCC recently completed a three-million-dollar upgrade to the Center. The 2011
upgrades included bringing all restrooms into ADA compliance, a cosmetic renovation of Andrews
Hall, the installation of an elevator, a new roof, and new heating/cooling systems. Prior upgrades
included renovation of the arts, dance and studio spaces, as well as the conversion of a classroom
into a business center with cubicles available for rent on a short-term basis.

For many years, the City Council has contributed funding to key non-profits to support their
operations and their provision of services to the community. Traditionally, the City's funding focus
has supported four non-profits, including the SCC, that provide core recreational and community
services since the City does not operate an in-house recreation and community services department.

You also note that the City has set aside $205,000 for fiscal year 2017-2018 funding for four
non-profits, support for community events, and a discretionary fund. Funding for the non-profits is
for current operations and services; it is not to be used for any facility improvements or upgrades.
Each of the four non-profits, including the SCC, provides services to residents of the City
(approximately 11,000 in population) and the unincorporated area of Sonoma Valley
(approximately 35,000 in population). City staff is recommending three-year contracts with each
non-profit, and is recommending the following funding for the SCC: $35,000 for fiscal year 2017-
2018; $40,000 for fiscal year 2018-2019; and $40,000 for fiscal year 2019-2020.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of the Act if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's interests, including an interest in real property. (Section 87103.)

A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation
18701(b).) The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly
involved in the decision. Vice Mayor Harrington's real property is not explicitly involved in the
decision. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than
hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. (Id.) If the financial result cannot be
expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not

File No. A-18-029
Page No. 3

reasonably foreseeable. Under Regulation 18702.2(a), a governmental decision's effect on real property is material if the decision:²

(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest;

(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than commercial property containing a business entity where the materiality standards are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property; or

(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property.

Given the facts presented, including the fact that the SCC facilities are already used for a wide variety of programs and that funds are not to be used for any facility improvements or upgrades, there would not seem to be any significant changes to the character of the neighborhood that would impact the Vice Mayor's property, and none of the materiality standards set forth above are met. Therefore, Vice Mayor Harrington may participate in decisions regarding the granting of funds to the SCC.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:jgl

² Regulation 18702(b) cautions: "Notwithstanding Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5, the financial effect of a governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant."

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of the Act if it is 
• reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the 
public official's interests, ii;tcluding an interest in real property. (Section 87103.) 
A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation

reasonably foreseeable. Under Regulation 18702.2(a), a governmental decision's effect on real 
property is material if the decision:2 
( 10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, 
of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, 
privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors 
that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which 
the official has a financial interest; 
(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other 
than commercial property containing a business entity where the 
materiality standards are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. 
Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written 
advice allowing an official to participate under these circumstances if 
the Commission detennines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that 
there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property; or 
(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due. care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental 
decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would 
influence the market value of the official's property. 
Given the facts presented, including the fact that the SCC facilities are already used for a

.

to the Sonoma Community Center (the "SCC") and an update of the City's community fund policy

within 500 feet of the property owned by the SCC?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Vice Mayor Harrington may participate in governmental decisions regarding the 
granting of funds to the SCC and an update to the City's community fund policy.
